64d93f114fa392c5b68c46cd.question: In one of this man’s poems, Strephon discovers that his beloved Celia “shits” after entering the title locale, a “Lady’s Dressing Room.” He uses the analogy of the Trojan horse “pregnant with Greeks” to describe Tories and Whigs huddled under a shed in “Description of a City Shower,” which is often paired with the urban georgic “A Description of the Morning.” He wrote a series of polemics against the monopoly on copper coinage granted to William Wood in Drapier’s Letters and his correspondence with Esther Johnson was collected in the Journal to Stella, although he is better known for a satire about Peter, Martin, and Jack and a novel whose hero was first a surgeon, then a captain of many ships. FTP, name this author of A Tale of a Tub and Gulliver’s Travels.

He uses the analogy of the Trojan horse "pregnant with Greeks" to describe Tories and Whigs huddled under a shed in "Description of a City Shower," which is often paired with the urban georgic "A Description of the Morning." He wrote a series of polemics against the monopoly on copper coinage granted to William Wood in Drapier's Letters and his correspondence with Esther Johnson was collected in the Journal to Stella, although he is better known for a satire about Peter, Martin, and Jack and a novel whose hero was first a surgeon, then a captain of many ships.
